content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 16 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1CO_016_page_001.jpg

### The Collection for Jerusalem

1. Now regarding your question about the money being collected for God's people in Jerusalem. You should follow the same procedure I gave to the churches in Galatia.

2. On the first day of each week, you should each put aside a portion of the money you have earned. Don't wait until I get there and then try to collect it all at once.

3. When I come, I will write letters of recommendation for the messengers you choose to deliver your gift to Jerusalem.

4. And if it seems appropriate for me to go along, they can travel with me.

### Paul's Travel Plans

5. I am coming to visit you after I have been to Macedonia, for I am planning to travel through Macedonia.

6. Perhaps I will stay awhile with you, possibly all winter. Then you can send me on my way to my next destination.

7. This time I don't want to make just a short visit and then go right on. I want to come and stay awhile, if the Lord will let me.

8. In the meantime, I will be staying here at Ephesus until the Festival of Pentecost.

9. There is a wide-open door for a great work here, although many oppose me.

10. When Timothy comes, don't intimidate him. He is doing the Lord's work, just as I am.

11. Don't let anyone treat him with contempt. Send him on his way with your blessing when he returns to me. I am expecting him to come with the other believers.

12. Now about our brother Apollos — I urged him to visit you with the other believers, but he was not willing to go right now. He will see you later when he has the opportunity.

### Paul's Final Instructions

13. Be on guard. Stand firm in the faith. Be courageous. Be strong.

14. And do everything with love.

15. You know that Stephanas and his household were the first of the harvest of believers in Greece, and they are spending their lives in service to God's people. I urge you, dear brothers and sisters,

16. to submit to them and others like them who serve with such devotion.

17. I am glad that Stephanas, Fortunatus, and Achaicus have come here. They have been providing the help you weren't here to give me.

18. They have been a wonderful encouragement to me, as they have been to you. You should give them your full recognition.

### Paul's Greetings

19. The churches here in the province of Asia send greetings in the Lord, as do Aquila and Priscilla and all the others who gather in their home for church meetings.

20. All the brothers and sisters here send greetings to you. Greet each other with a sacred kiss.

21. Here is my greeting in my own handwriting — Paul.

22. If anyone does not love the Lord, that person is cursed. Our Lord, come!

23. May the grace of the Lord Jesus be with you.

24. My love to all of you in Christ Jesus.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verses 1-4 — The collection:** After sixteen chapters of theology, Paul ends with a request for money. But this is not ordinary fundraising. The collection for the Jerusalem church was Paul's most ambitious practical project — a gift from Gentile churches to the Jewish mother church. It was theology made tangible: if the Gentiles share in Israel's spiritual blessings, they should share their material blessings in return (Romans 15:27). The collection was Paul's proof that the unity he preached was real.

**Verse 2 — "The first day of every week":** This is the earliest evidence that Christians gathered on Sunday. Why Sunday? Because Jesus rose on the first day of the week. Every Sunday gathering is a mini-Easter celebration.

**Verse 9 — "A wide-open door...and many who oppose me":** This is Paul's reality in miniature. Every great opportunity comes with great opposition. The door is open — and the enemies are real. That's not a contradiction; that's how the gospel works.

**Verse 13 — Four commands:** "Be on guard. Stand firm. Be courageous. Be strong." These are military commands — the language of soldiers on duty. After all the theological depth of this letter, Paul ends with simple, urgent imperatives. The Christian life is not a seminar — it's a battle.

**Verse 14 — "Do everything with love":** One sentence that summarizes the entire letter. After sixteen chapters addressing divisions, immorality, lawsuits, marriage, idol food, worship disorder, gift competition, and resurrection denial — the final instruction is this. Do everything with love. That's it. That's the whole letter.

**Verse 22 — "Marana tha":** An Aramaic prayer preserved in a Greek letter sent to a Roman colony in Greece. "Our Lord, come!" This little phrase connects Corinth to Jerusalem, Greek speakers to Aramaic speakers, the most sophisticated theological letter to the simplest prayer of the earliest believers. Come, Lord Jesus.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
